macro_segments.title: The Mishnah on Conditional Nazirut: When a Son is Born Before Day Seventy

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the Mishnah: Nazirut that takes effect conditionally and sequentially
The principle of double-counting: when the son's nazirut is completely contained within the first hundred days
The boundary at day seventy: if the son is born up to day seventy, no loss; after day seventy, hiatus
Tosafot's interpretation: the significance of 'tiglachas' and the thirty-day minimum for nazirut

macro_segments.title: Rav's Radical View: Day Seventy Double-Counts (Oleh L'kan U'lekan)

micro_segments.title: Rav's position: day seventy itself counts for both the first nazirut and as day one for the son
Tosafot's explanation: how double-counting day seventy permits bringing the korban on day one hundred
The Gemara's challenge to Rav: if day seventy counts double, why is day seventy-one a problem?

macro_segments.title: The Gemara's Refutation of Rav's View Through the Mishnah's Language

micro_segments.title: The Gemara's logic: if day seventy double-counts, the Mishnah should say 'he gains' not 'he loses nothing'
The Gemara's reading: 'achar' must mean day seventy-two, not day seventy-one, proving the son's day doesn't begin until day seventy-two
Conclusion: the day the son is born cannot count as his first day because it is already part of the first nazirut

macro_segments.title: Clarification: Partial Days in Nazirut vs. Double-Counting

micro_segments.title: General principle: if a person declares nazirut today with only an hour left, today counts as day one
Why day seventy-one doesn't work: the day has already begun as part of the first nazirut before the son is born

macro_segments.title: Rav's Support in Abba Shaul's View on Mourning and the Concept of Miktzas HaYom

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the principle of miktzas hayom k'kulo from the laws of aveilus (mourning)
The Mishnah in Moed Katan: minimum three days of aveilus before Yom Tov interrupts shiva
Abba Shaul's radical view: no need for a haircut; day seven already counts as beginning the shloshim period
How Abba Shaul's principle of day seven double-counting parallels Rav's idea in nazirut

macro_segments.title: The Problem: Rav's View is Supported by Abba Shaul, but Abba Shaul Only Applies to Rabbinic Laws

micro_segments.title: The Gemara's concern: how can we extend Abba Shaul's principle from rabbinic aveilus to the Torah-level concept of nazirut?
Rabbi Yosi's position on the zava (menstruant woman): a full day is only needed if it doesn't contain bleeding at its midpoint

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Yosi on the Zava: The Woman Who Bleeds on Day Two

micro_segments.title: The case: a woman observes one clean day opposite a day she saw blood, and they bring a Pesach offering for her on that clean day
The problem: if she bleeds again later that same day, does the Pesach offering still count or is she retroactively impure?
Rabbi Yosi's ruling: the Pesach offering counts because the partial day suffices; any later bleeding is a new impurity

macro_segments.title: The Gemara's Analysis: Two Models of Miktzas HaYom

micro_segments.title: Application to day seventy-one: under the sufficiency model, the first hours count for the first nazirut and the later hours for the son's
Model Two: partial day is sufficient in itself; what happens later in the day is entirely separate and new
Model One: partial day counts only in anticipation that the rest of the day will work out properly

macro_segments.title: The Gemara's Question: Retroactive Impurity—Rabbinic or Biblical?

micro_segments.title: First approach: Rabbi Yosi holds that retroactive impurity (if she bleeds later) is only rabbinic, not biblical
Second approach: Rabbi Yosi holds retroactive impurity is biblical, but the Pesach offering is a special exception (tumah of the depths)
The concept of tumah of the depths: impurity that is unknowable at the time the offering is brought

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Yoshiya's Opinion: Day Seven Can Be Retroactively Disrupted

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yoshiya's case: if a zav has an emission on day seven, he loses all seven days and must restart
Rabbi Yochanan's response: no, only day seven itself is lost, not all seven days
The Gemara's logical problem: if partial day works as sufficiency model, the emission shouldn't matter at all

macro_segments.title: Resolving the Logical Problem: Rabbi Yochanan's Support for the Sufficiency Model

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yochanan's principle: if a zav goes to the mikvah in the morning on day seven, the emission later that day does not retroactively affect it
The support for Rabbi Yosi and Rav: what matters is 'from now onward' a new impurity begins; the past is unaffected
Rabbi Oshaya's conclusion: Rabbi Yosi aligns with Rabbi Yochanan on the principle that later events do not retroactively affect earlier parts of the day

macro_segments.title: The Final Question: How Does a Woman Ever Become a Full Zava Under Rabbi Yosi's Model?

micro_segments.title: The problem: if every day can be split in half by a partial day of purity, how do three consecutive bleeding days occur?
Solution one: she bleeds three days consecutively without any break whatsoever
Solution two: the bleeding straddles the sunset in a way that neither the beginning nor the end of each day is clean
Conclusion: miktzas hayom requires either a beginning or an end of the day to be clean in order to function as a break

topical_tags.term: Conditional nazirut (hareini nazir k'sheyihiyeh li ben)
Hiatus and suspension in sequential nazirut
Rav vs. Mishnah: gains vs. loses nothing
Partial vs. full day in counting
Miktzas hayom k'kulo (partial day counts as full)
Derabbanan vs. de'oraita (rabbinic vs. Torah law)
Rabbi Yosi on zava and miktzas hayom
Tumah of the depths (tumah that is unknowable)
Rabbi Yochanan's response to Rabbi Yoshiya
Mikan u'lehava metamei (from now onward impurity)
